submission.title: What are your first thoughts when you hear someone call themselves an “Alpha”?

comments.body: Asshole.
If you have to announce that your in charge/competent/capable/better/alpha/whatever youre not that thing.
In my experience a true alpha individual never announces themselves with words.

"Yikes" 
While also internally cringing
Cringe.
Cries in their bed at night.
If you're announcing it you're probably a litte less alpha than you imagined.

If he's not leading something, if he is not a leader,  he is not an alpha, by definition. 

A set of descriptors ie. 

>one who observes, sits, doesn’t get mad, Is humble, understands and judges internally.

Does not make someone an alpha. In our society an alpha, by definition, is... a ceo, chairman of the board, an elected official, at the very least a leading influencer in a social group. That's what ther word "alpha" means. The person who is in charge, dominating all the others, leading the cohort. Regardless of their personal qualities or how they got there. 

Most people claiming to be alphas are not, as they're not really in charge or in control of anything or anyone. Like Andrew tate for instance. I mean, he might be the alpha of his small friend group and family, but not of anything meaningful. 

Unless you redefine the word?
You will NEVER hear a real Alpha call themselves an "Alpha", we don't have to!
I'm a manchild
Small pecker… 😂
that they're trying waaaaay too hard.
How to admit to being a complete douchebag without saying "I'm a total douchebag!"

But i compare calling yourself that to women that call themselves "a strong independent woman". 

If you have to announce it or claim it....you probably aren't it.
Not alpha.

Look at the previous 5 incarnations of this question for my answer.
Absolutely not an alpha, but tries desperately–and incorrectly–to prove that they are.

This question insinuates that people unironically calling themselves alpha is something that occurs in real life, which I'm not entirely convinced it does. I've never observed this happen and the last thing an "alpha" male by definition would find themselves doing is calling themselves an alpha--that would undermine their inherent alphaness.

With that said, if I DID catch someone referring to themselves as an alpha, I would think they are approaching the idea from an overly theoretical angle, likely because they are young and just barely figuring out their understanding of masculinity. They hear "alpha" thrown around a lot online so they just parrot the term in conversation if they don't know any better.
Same thoughts I get when someone calls themselves a guru.....

Interesting question! When I hear someone call themselves an 'Alpha', my first thoughts are usually mixed. On one hand, I understand that some people may use the term to convey confidence, assertiveness, and leadership qualities. It can be seen as a way to express self-assurance and a go-getter attitude.

On the other hand, I also recognize that the concept of "Alpha" comes from outdated social hierarchies and animal behavior studies that may not accurately reflect human behavior. It can be seen as promoting toxic masculinity and an overly competitive mindset, as it implies that there are winners and losers in social interactions.

It's important to remember that human behavior is complex and cannot be reduced to simplistic labels like "Alpha" or "Beta". Everyone has unique strengths and qualities that make them valuable, and it's important to respect and appreciate the diversity of personalities and characteristics in our society.

In conclusion, while the term "Alpha" may have different connotations for different people, I believe it's important to approach it with caution and consider the broader context in which it is being used. What are your thoughts on this topic?

submission.title: Major switch from CE to EE
submission.body: Hi guys, hope you all are doing well. I am currently in my sophomore year of BS Computer Engineering and was planning on switching to Electrical Engineering over prospects. Any tips would be appreciated. Anything at all related to EE :D

comments.body: It's insanely fun if you can visualise abstract concepts like current flow and voltage rise and drops but once get the hang of it is unbelievably fun

I would advise sticking with CE while focusing on the analog / hardware side because getting exposure to programming and tool usage is invaluable now, unless you are dead set on doing more math. 

If you do switch, make sure you feel good with your calculus, probability, and math identities abilities. Essentially, you are trading doing less programming for more pen-and-paper math.

thing is my university offers courses which do not really cater to hardware if u are CE (it is hard to explain) which is 1 of the reasons i considered switching. Plus i have had people tell me i can always take up CE electives as an EE major so i was considering that option
oooh tysm! any sites/course material i should review? I will have Basic ELectronics, Signals and Systems as two core nezt semester
Go for EE then
alright, tysm!! i will def think it through again :D

Revise up on how inductors and capacitors operate and circuit analysis maybe it helps more than u think.
One book I liked a lot was sadiku can't remember the full name but it was pretty good for the basics
I did exactly this (change from CE to EE) but didn’t really change anything since the degrees were so similar
I get what you mean is that they teach you more embedded and OS, software rather than hardware/vlsi stuff 🤔

Depending on your credits, add a CS minor to your EE major. I switched from CE to EE and all I needed to do was take a computer architecture class that completed my CS minor as well counted as an EE elective class. I would recommend remembering how to integrate because I did not integrate for like 2 years and then did pretty bad on an exam for stats and was struggling for a week in a RF class (multivariable calc). Might just be a me problem tho.
If your good at math you’ll have a breeze, it’s 75% code

submission.title: Crash Course before Finals hit
submission.body:
Hi everyone, i hope you are all well. I need some help. Right so i havent done all that well on calc 1 so far and im barely passing right now.

Any resources online, at ALL, that could help me learn/practice calc faster? all the textbooks we use either by stewart or hughes hallet etc, do not have solutions online (or are paid) so any help rn would be appreciated as i just have november to recover from this untill finals week in december. Pls helpp <3

edit: calc 1 for my uni covers the main topics of partially pre-calc material, limits/continuity etc, differntiability, integrals, sequences and series

comments.body:
As a reminder...

Posts asking for help on homework questions **require**:

* **the complete problem statement**,

* **a genuine attempt at solving the problem, which may be either computational, or a discussion of ideas or concepts you believe may be in play**,

* **question is not from a current exam or quiz**.

Commenters responding to homework help posts **should not do OP’s homework for them**.

Please see [this page](https://www.reddit.com/r/calculus/wiki/homeworkhelp) for the further details regarding homework help posts.

**If you are asking for general advice about your current calculus class, please be advised that simply referring your class as “Calc *n*“ is not entirely useful, as “Calc *n*” may differ between different colleges and universities. In this case, please refer to your class syllabus or college or university’s course catalogue for a listing of topics covered in your class, and include that information in your post rather than assuming everybody knows what will be covered in your class.**

*I am a bot, and this action was performed automatically. Please [contact the moderators of this subreddit](/message/compose/?to=/r/calculus) if you have any questions or concerns.*
Have you tried office hours?
i wont lie. Only a couple of times have i tried office hours because its like i get stuff during classes but when i solve practice problems i feel lost.
Go to office hours with said practice problems. Knowing theory is different from application and a teacher can help

how long till exam? if its 1 month till, (for me its  1 month 8 days)

you could watch through professor leonards playlist within a week if you dedicate to it.

that is about 8-10 hours of videos every day. but you will understand it

[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fYyARMqiaag&list=PLF797E961509B4EB5](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fYyARMqiaag&list=PLF797E961509B4EB5)
